sessions.title: Session 1 — Foundational Assessment: Footwork, Grip, Swing & Rally
sessions.takeaway: This first session was a full assessment to understand Anamithraa's current level and set a clear direction for training. The encouraging part is that the building blocks are already there — solid swing preparation, a working shuffle step, and naturally good drop shots. The main focus going forward is generating power from the body and a proper swing rather than the arm, sharpening footwork timing, and tightening up movement at the net. The two homework drills target the most important foundations first: footwork and forearm pronation.
sessions.summary: Session 1 was a comprehensive assessment across footwork, grip, swing mechanics, and live rallying. Coach Z began with shadow movement footwork to the front, middle, and rear court, then checked Anamithraa's racket grip and overhead swing. The session finished with a few live rallies to see how everything holds together under real play. The findings give a simple roadmap: build a stronger overhead clear by driving power from the body and a pronated swing, improve footwork timing, and develop more compact movement at the net — which together will also make her already-good drop shots far more effective.

observations.text: Decent swing preparation and shuffle step right from the start — a solid foundation to build on.
When her footwork looks slow, it is usually a timing issue — the timing of the split step, push-off, and lowering her center of gravity — rather than a lack of raw foot speed.

Her overhead clear currently reaches at most the middle of the court. This is mostly because she relies on her arm to hit the shuttle instead of generating power from a full, pronated swing and the body.
She has good drop shots but tends to overuse them. As her clear improves, the drop will become far more effective because opponents will have to respect the threat of the clear.

She makes a big, unnecessary movement at the front court when trying to play a net shot or lift. Tightening this into a more compact, controlled movement will improve both speed and consistency at the net.

drills.name: Shadow Movement Footwork — Front, Middle & Rear Court
drills.description: Shadow footwork without a shuttle, covering movement to the front court, mid court, and rear court. This builds the movement patterns and court coverage that everything else is layered on top of.
drills.notes: Focus on the timing of the split step and push-off, and on staying low through the movement rather than just trying to move fast.

drills.name: Grip & Swing Check
drills.description: Coach Z checked Anamithraa's racket grip and overhead swing mechanics to establish a technical baseline. The key finding is a tendency to generate power from the arm rather than from a full, pronated swing using the forearm and body.
drills.notes: The goal over the next few sessions is to shift from an arm-driven hit to a relaxed, pronated swing where power comes from forearm rotation and the body — this is what will extend the clear to the back of the court.

drills.name: Live Rally with Coach Z
drills.description: A few live rallies with Coach Z to see how footwork, swing, and shot selection hold up in real play. This is where the assessment came together and the training priorities became clear.
drills.notes: Good early sign: the swing preparation and shuffle step already show up naturally in rallies. The next layers are clear depth, footwork timing, and tighter net movement.

tips.text: Power comes from the body and the swing, not the arm. A relaxed, pronated overhead swing will send the clear to the back of the court far more easily than muscling it with the arm.
Footwork speed is mostly timing. Sharpen the split step and push-off, stay low, and the movement will feel faster without trying to rush.
Keep movement at the net small and controlled. A compact step into the shuttle is quicker and more consistent than a big reach.

homework.name: Dynamic Footwork Drill
homework.description: An at-home dynamic footwork drill to develop faster, better-timed movement around the court.
homework.notes: The more you do this drill — and it can be done at home — the more it translates into faster, sharper footwork on court.

homework.name: Forearm Pronation in Overhead Swings
homework.description: A drill to develop forearm pronation on overhead swings, so power comes from the rotation of the forearm and body rather than the arm alone.
homework.notes: We briefly covered pronation in the first class, but it is still natural to fall back on swinging with the arm. Practicing this at home will start to build the correct overhead motion.

sessions.homeworkNote: Both drills can be done at home. These are fundamental building blocks — getting footwork timing and a proper pronated swing in place now is what makes faster footwork and more advanced skills possible later.

sessions.title: 2026 US Junior National Championship
sessions.summary: Match-play analysis from the July 8–14 tournament, with Coach Z's observations and the near-term training progression.
sessions.coachComment: Anamithraa did well to win the first game, but she became over-reliant on her drop shots. Once her opponent adjusted and began waiting at the front of the court, the opponent had a much smaller area to cover while the court became much larger for Anamithraa. She was also moving before her opponent started serving, which compromised her timing. Another important growth area is the overhead rear-court corner, where she lost many points.
sessions.trainingPlan: Forearm pronation → hit the clear consistently with enough height and distance → split step → footwork combining clear and net → overhead rear-court corner → cover the full court with control

sessions.title: Session 2 — Building the Clear: Forearm Pronation & Timing
sessions.takeaway: Today we started Anamithraa's personalized training plan by building a stronger clear. Her forearm pronation and clear consistency improved as the session went on, especially in the final rally. The next step is to repeat the motion at home, get behind the shuttle early, and learn to use the sound of contact as feedback.
sessions.summary: This session focused on adding a reliable overhead clear to Anamithraa's game. The progression started with seated forearm-pronation work, moved into coach-fed shuttles from a sideways preparation, and finished with clear rallies. She still occasionally forced the shot with a larger arm-and-body effort, but her timing and consistency improved toward the end of the session.

observations.text: The seated drill looked encouraging overall and also served as a homework check. Anamithraa can produce the pronation movement, but it is not automatic yet; she occasionally returns to forcing the shot with her arm and body instead of using relaxed forearm rotation.

Her sideways preparation and forearm pronation looked solid during the coach-fed shuttle drill. Getting underneath or slightly behind the shuttle earlier will help her contact it from a stronger position.
The clear rallies improved toward the end. Her final rally was the most consistent, showing that repetition and feedback during the session were already helping.
A clean clear has a different sound from a forced or mistimed clear. Learning to hear that difference will help Anamithraa recognize the quality of her own shot and make adjustments during a game.

drills.name: Seated Forearm Pronation & Homework Check
drills.description: Anamithraa sat on a chair to keep her body quiet and isolate the overhead arm action. This made it easier to focus on forearm pronation and gave Coach Z a clear look at how much of the Session 1 homework had carried over.
drills.notes: The motion is developing well, but it still needs more repetition at home. Keep the swing relaxed and let the forearm rotate through contact instead of trying to push or muscle the shuttle.

drills.name: Coach Feed — Sideways Preparation to Clear
drills.description: Coach Z fed shuttles while Anamithraa began each repetition sideways, prepared her overhead swing, and added forearm pronation through contact.
drills.notes: The swing looked good overall. Move early and position yourself underneath or slightly behind the shuttle so you can contact it from an optimal position before hitting the clear.

drills.name: Clear Rally with Coach Z
drills.description: Anamithraa and Coach Z rallied using clears to build rhythm, height, distance, and repeatable technique. Clear rallies will remain a regular part of future sessions so the shot becomes dependable in games.
drills.notes: The clear became more consistent toward the end, with the final rally showing the best rhythm. Watch both clips and listen carefully to the sound of each contact — a clean clear sounds different from a forced or mistimed one.

tips.text: Move first, then hit. Get underneath or slightly behind the shuttle so your body and racket are in a strong position for the clear.
Keep the swing relaxed. Forearm rotation creates cleaner power than trying to muscle the shuttle with a big arm-and-body effort.
Listen to your racket. The sound of a clean clear can tell you whether the contact was good, even before you see where the shuttle lands.

homework.name: Dynamic Footwork Drill
homework.description: An at-home dynamic footwork drill to develop faster, better-timed movement around the court.
homework.notes: This is the main homework priority before Session 3. The more you practice this drill at home, the more it will help your split-step timing, movement rhythm, and readiness for the next stage of training.

homework.name: Forearm Pronation in Overhead Swings
homework.description: A drill to develop forearm pronation on overhead swings, so power comes from the rotation of the forearm and body rather than the arm alone.
homework.notes: Continue building the relaxed forearm rotation from Session 1 and Session 2. Focus on smooth, correct repetitions rather than trying to swing harder.

sessions.homeworkNote: Main focus before Session 3: Dynamic Footwork Drill. This drill will prepare Anamithraa's split-step timing and movement rhythm for the next session. Please also continue the forearm-pronation drill so her clear keeps becoming more natural and consistent.
